original_abstract: This chapter examines the disconnect between the democratic, equitable ideals espoused by Canadian public schooling and the restrictive reality of anti-violence and conflict management programs in urban settings. Drawing on qualitative research, the study reveals that most school-based initiatives prioritize "negative peace"—a state of surveillance, coercive discipline, and exclusionary peacekeeping—over substantive, democratic peacebuilding. These practices often reflect the cultural biases of privileged populations and are increasingly reinforced by systemic budget cuts and curriculum standardization. By emphasizing immediate behavioral compliance rather than addressing the structural roots of injustice, such approaches frequently exacerbate racialized and gendered social inequalities. The analysis suggests that unless schools move beyond mere peacekeeping to embrace transformative dialogue and critical pedagogy, they risk legitimizing the very patterns of marginalization and systemic violence they ostensibly aim to prevent.
humanized_abstract: Chapter 2 “Incompatible Ideas” In this chapter we seek to explain the disconnect between the ideals that underlie the rhetoric of equality and democracy that underpins Canadian public schooling and the prohibition on peace from the “wrong” side of the gate that young people encounter when they are involved in anti-violence and conflict management programs in schools in Toronto’s inner city. Using data from the case studies, we explore why schooling at the very sites where young people experience considerable adversity to peace ends up being focused on maintaining a variant of negative peace that ensures that any transgressions are detected, punishment meted out and offenders rendered peaceful, if not safe, through mechanisms of intense surveillance, disciplinary techniques and peacekeeping that privileges the cultural worldview of those who are privileged. Moreover, these schooling practices reflect the times in which these activities are occurring (e.g. during a period of substantial cuts to publicly funded social programs) and the growing imposition of a common curriculum upon divergent instructional approaches. School peace initiatives that limit their activities to enforcement of prohibitions on deviance rather than creating the conditions in which young people and their adult caretakers are empowered to explore their understandings of the sources of violence in their communities in a shared way that is designed to raise critical awareness and generate solutions to the sources of that violence risk legitimizing those very structures of inequality that schooling on its most abstract level promises to remove. In other words the enforcement of schooling without any opportunities for participatory dialogue in which young people, people can work out whether peace as envisioned by those with power is what is wanted by those on the margins of societal life maintains rather than challenges existing patterns of racism and sexism.
